PurpleCrayon's blog

Codeforces Round #728 Editorial
By PurpleCrayon, history, 5 years ago,

1541A - Симпатичные перестановки

Author: PurpleCrayon

Hint

The parity (whether nn is odd or even) matters.

Hint

Swapping two adjacent cats keeps both of them close to their original location and changes both of their locations.

Hint

If nn is even, the optimal distance is nn, and if nn is odd the optimal distance is n+1n+1.

Solution

1541A - Pretty Permutations

There are two cases:

  • if nn is even, print: [2,1,4,3,6,5…n,n−1][2,1,4,3,6,5…n,n−1] Formally, you swap every other pair of adjacent elements. This is optimal because the total distance is nn, which has to be minimal since the distance of one cat must be ≥1≥1.
  • if nn is odd, first print [3,1,2][3,1,2] then solve the even case for the remaining elements. This is optimal because the distance is n+1n+1, which has to be minimal since a distance of nn is not achievable.

1541B - Приятные пары

Author: PurpleCrayon

Hint

i+j≤2⋅ni+j≤2⋅n

Hint

The number of pairs (a,b)(a,b) such that a⋅b≤xa⋅b≤x is O(xlogx)O(xlogx).

Solution

1541B - Pleasant Pairs

Loop over all values of aiai and ajaj. Because i+j≤2⋅ni+j≤2⋅n, we only care about pairs (ai,aj)(ai,aj) if ai⋅aj≤2⋅nai⋅aj≤2⋅n. The number of such pairs is O(nlogn)O(nlogn), so you can brute force all pairs.

The reason the total number of pairs is O(nlogn)O(nlogn) is because if the first element of the pair is xx, there are only 2⋅nx2⋅nx possible values of yy. 2⋅n1+2⋅n2+2⋅n3+…2⋅nn=2⋅n(11+12+13…1n)=O(nlogn)2⋅n1+2⋅n2+2⋅n3+…2⋅nn=2⋅n(11+12+13…1n)=O(nlogn) by the harmonic series. Thus the solution runs in O(nlogn)O(nlogn) time total.

1540A - Отличные графы

Author: PurpleCrayon

Hint

What's the minimum value that an edge from aa to bb can be?

Hint

Use edges with negative value whenever you can.

Hint

The sum of the values of edges with positive weight must be ≥≥ the maximum value in the array.

Solution

1540A - Great Graphs

Note that if there are two nodes aa and bb and you want to add an edge between them, the value of the edge must be ≥db−da≥db−da. Otherwise, the cows could take a path to bb that goes through dada that's strictly less than dbdb. With this in mind, let's add all edges (a,b)(a,b) with weight db−dadb−da if and only if db≤dadb≤da. All of these numbers are not positive, which means they can't make our answer worse. They also don't change the shortest paths, from our first observation. Now, let's call the node with the max didi node xx. You can add a single edge from node 11 to node xx with cost dxdx, and now the graph is good. This is because node xx is already connected to all other nodes, which means there is always a shortest path to some node aa with the right length by going from 1→x→a1→x→a. However, naively looping through all pairs is too slow. Instead, you can sort dd and calculate the contribution of each node to the answer. The complexity is O(nlogn)O(nlogn).

1540B - Древесный массив

Author: ijxjdjd

Hint

Fix the initial node chosen and root the tree there, what is the contribution of each pair of nodes?

Hint

Nothing matters besides the path from node aa to node bb, and the initially chosen node rr.

Hint

You are given two stacks of size a1a1 and a2a2. In a single step, you randomly choose a stack to remove a single item from. What is the probability that a1a1 becomes 00 before a2a2? Can you extend this argument for an arbitrary probability pp to remove from one of the two stacks, and a probability of 1−2p1−2p to do nothing?

Solution

1540B - Tree Array

Parsing through the problem statement, the process can be seen as choosing a starting node and "expanding" the subtree of marked nodes to nodes adjacent to the marked component.

Fixing a given root rr, the expected value of the entire process is obviously the sum of the expected values for a fixed root divided by nn.

Find the contribution of the inversion of two nodes (a,b)(a,b) where a<ba<b. The expected contribution for any pair (a,b)(a,b) is equal to the probability that bb appears before aa with a given root.

Set l=lca(a,b)l=lca(a,b). Note that, until reaching ll, every possible process still has the same probability of reaching bb before aa as it did when the first node was chosen. Therefore, we can assume that the process has reached ll and calculate the probability from there.

Once ll is reached, we now note that the probability that the process "gets closer" to bb is always equal to the probability of getting closer to aa.

The problem can be rephrased as having two stacks of size dist(l,a)dist(l,a) and dist(l,b)dist(l,b) with an arbitrary pp to remove a node from one of the two stack (and 1−2p1−2p to nothing) and finding the probability that dist(l,b)dist(l,b) reaches zero before dist(l,a)dist(l,a).

However, it turns out that the actual probability pp does not matter. We propose a function F[x][y]F[x][y] that defines the probability that a stack of size yy becomes 00 before a stack of size xx. In fact a function exists and it is defined as F[x][y]=F[x−1][y]+F[x][y−1]2F[x][y]=F[x−1][y]+F[x][y−1]2. Intuitively, this is because the probability of decreasing xx or decreasing yy is always the same, so the probability of transitioning the state we end up transitioning to is always the same, regardless of pp.

So, the solution is clear. Iterate over and root at all nodes. Then at the given root, iterate over all pairs of node a<ba<b and add F[dist(l,a)][dist(l,b)]F[dist(l,a)][dist(l,b)] to the answer. Finally, divide by nn. In total, the solution works in O(N3logN)O(N3log⁡N) or O(N3)O(N3).

1540C2 - Сходящийся массив (сложная версия)

Author: ijxjdjd

Hint

What are the invariants? The monovariants?

Hint

What is the prefix sum array of the converged array? What is the difference array of the converged array?

Hint

What if you conducted the process on a prefix of size 11, then of size 22, and on and on?

Hint

How many values xx are actually interesting?

Solution

1540C2 - Converging Array (Hard Version)

First, reduce the operations into something more manageable. It turns out operation ii sets ai+1−ai=max(bi,ai+1−ai)ai+1−ai=max(bi,ai+1−ai) while keeping ai+1+aiai+1+ai constant. Visually, this is simultaneously moving aiai up and ai+1ai+1 down until ai+1−a[i]≥biai+1−a[i]≥bi.

Define ff to be the final converged array.

Let's some observations

  1. If ai+1−ai=biai+1−ai=bi is ever satisfied throughout the process (if an operation ever moves anything), fi+1−fi=bifi+1−fi=bi. Equivalently, if fi+1−fi>bifi+1−fi>bi then no operation ii could have ever been conducted
  2. If no operation ii has been conducted, then [1,i][1,i] is independent of [i+1,n][i+1,n].
  3. If ii is the first operation that has never been conducted, then ∑ij=1aj=∑ij=1fj∑j=1iaj=∑j=1ifj because no sum could have been exchanged between aiai and ai+1ai+1.

Let's assume that we know that ii is the first observation that hasn't been conducted. We can then restore f1f1 because we know that f1+f1+b1+f1+b1+b2…=a1+a2+…+aif1+f1+b1+f1+b1+b2…=a1+a2+…+ai. To keep the tutorial clean, we refer to the prefix of prefix summation of bibi as bpibpi and the prefix summation of aiai as apiapi. Namely, we can solve for f1=api−bpiif1=api−bpii given that ii is the first observation never conducted.

It turns out that f1=min(api−bpii)f1=min(api−bpii) over all ii. This can be shown to be a lower bound on f1f1 because the answer is obviously in this set as there must be some ii that is the first operation never conducted. This can also be shown to be an upperbound on the answer by playing the game on each prefix ii. At each prefix ii, the term api−bpiiapi−bpii is an upperbound because, if it's not equal to that term, there must be some fi+1−fi>bifi+1−fi>bi so f1<api−bpiif1<api−bpii because f1+f2+…+fif1+f2+…+fi remains the same.

Returning to the actual problem, we need to count arrays min(api−bpii)≥xmin(api−bpii)≥x. In other words, api≥i⋅x+bpiapi≥i⋅x+bpi must hold for all ii. Let's do dynamic programming on apiapi. Call dp[i][api]dp[i][api] the number of prefixes of length ii with current prefix sum of apiapi. We can transition to i+1i+1 from ii using prefix sums on each valid apiapi.

Define M=max(ai)M=max(ai). The current complexity is O(QMN2)O(QMN2) The final step is noticing that there are only O(M)O(M) valid integer positions that end up being important for f1f1. Intuitively, this is because in nearly all cases every operation ii ends up being used. To rigorously prove, let's find an upperbound on relevant xx. If M⋅n<x⋅n+bpiM⋅n<x⋅n+bpi then there are 00 valid arrays. Because x⋅n+bpix⋅n+bpi is concave and negative on the decreasing portion (i.e. the function goes down immediately into negative if it ever becomes negative, otherwise strictly increases), we can draw the inequality 0≥x⋅n+bpi0≥x⋅n+bpi, otherwise every array ends up being good. Reducing the inequalities, we can realize that there is exactly MM different possible solutions. So, we can precalculate in O(M2N2)O(M2N2) and answer in O(1)O(1).

1540D - Инвертируем инверсии

Author: PurpleCrayon

Hint

The answer is always unique.

Hint

Try to figure out what the location of the ii-th element would be if you only looked at the first ii elements, then the first i+1i+1, etc. to find an O(nq)O(nq) solution.

Hint

Use sqrt decomposition to optimize it.

Solution

1540D - Inverse Inversions

We'll assume the arrays and the final permutation are 0-indexed from this point forward (you can shift values accordingly at the end).

Let's start with calculating the final array, without any updates. Let cici be the number of indices jj such that pj<pipj<pi and i<ji<j. It is easy to see that ci=i−bici=i−bi. Now imagine sweeping from left to right, maintaining the array aa. Let's say you're currently at index ii, and you have a list of all indices ≤i≤i, where the location of some index jj is the value of ajaj. You know that ii must be the cici-th out of those (after inserting it into the list) as the cici smallest values must be before it. This means that you can insert index ii at the ci−thci−th position in the list, and you need to find the final location of index ii in each query.

Now, let's support O(1)O(1) updates and O(n)O(n) queries. The first thing to note is that you don't need the entire array aa in each query, you just need to query some specific element. Assume you're querying the ii-th element of the array. Let's repeat the algorithm mentioned above except instead of storing the lists, you only store the location of the ii-th element for each query. Now, you keep some variable locloc which stores the location of the ii-th element in the list. It is initialized to cici, as it is first inserted to the cici-th position. Now you sweep through all indices jj where j>ij>i, and check if locloc is changed by the insert. This is only true if loc≥a[j]loc≥a[j]. This allows for O(1)O(1) updates but O(n)O(n) queries, which is still too slow.

To speed this up, let's use sqrt-decomp. Divide the array into multiple blocks. For each block, let's store an array nextnext where nextlocnextloc represents the final value of the variable locloc if it passes through the block. If we have this array, you can loop over all of the blocks and "jump" over them using the next array, querying in O(n/B)O(n/B) time, where BB is the size of the block. But how do you calculate and update the nextnext array? Initially, you can calculate the next array in O(n⋅B)O(n⋅B), by going to each index and naively tracking its position. Updating it is harder. One observation to make is that for each index inside a block, there is some value such that if loc≥xloc≥x it will be changed by the index, otherwise it will not. You can find all of those values for a single block using a binary indexed tree: sweep from left to right, query for the the smallest update locloc using a lower bound on the BIT, and increment a suffix. Then, you can increment all of those suffixes in the next array using another BIT, which would lead to O(nn−−√+qn−−√logn)O(nn+qnlog⁡n), as queries are O((n/B)logn)O((n/B)log⁡n) and updates are O(Blogn)O(Blog⁡n), which will probably be too slow.

To make this faster, note that the suffix that each element is responsible changes by at most one for all non-updated elements. This means that you can update the next array in O(1)O(1) for these elements. However, the updated element's suffix might change by O(n)O(n). To account for this, you can use another square root decomposition on the next array, which allows for O(n−−√)O(n) range updates, O(1)O(1) point updates, and O(1)O(1) point queries. This means that updates will remain O(Blogn)O(Blog⁡n), but queries will become O(1)O(1), so the final complexity is O(qnlogn−−−−−√)O(qnlog⁡n) with the right blocksize, which is definitely fast enough (the model solution runs in 22 seconds).

If you know of any faster solutions (O(nn−−√)O(nn) or even O(n⋅logk)O(n⋅logk⁡) let us know down in the comments).

1540E - Вкусные блюда

Author: ijxjdjd

Hint

How much does each chef's initial dish contribute at time kk?

Hint

What if you really, really wanted to use matrix exponentiation?

Hint

How can you multiply some vectors by a matrix in O(N)O(N) time? (go back to linear algebra class)

Hint

Decompose into linear combinations of eigenvectors.

Solution

1540E - Tasty Dishes

All operations are conducted under a modulo, it can be proven that each operation we conduct is valid within the modulo.

Key Idea 1 It's optimal to perform each operation if the number being added/multiplied is strictly positive. Specifically, it's optimal to do ai:=i⋅aiai:=i⋅ai and ai:=ai+a+jai:=ai+a+j iif ai>0ai>0 and aj>0aj>0 respectively.

Key Idea 2 A chef's dish ii becomes positive after the dd'th day where dd is the closest distance of chef ii from a positive element. We call this value didi for the ii'th chef. Initial observations, there are only O(N2)O(N2) pairs of (i,di)(i,di) where didi is not infinite (never reaches positive value and thus never changes).

Key Idea 3 We refer to a vector of length nn with n−1n−1 zeros and a single 11 at the ii'th index as e⃗ ie→i. If we assume that all chefs are currently positive then every chef takes every opportunity copy and the transition matrix TT is well defined and obvious.

We can represent the final array at time kk as a=Tk∑di≤kT−die⃗ iaia=Tk∑di≤kT−die→iai + ∑di>ke⃗ iai∑di>ke→iai. This immediately offers a O(QN2+N4)O(QN2+N4) potential solution with very good constant. Because TT is diagonal, its inverse can with respect to a vector v⃗ v→ be calculated in O(N2)O(N2). So, we can precalculate T−ke⃗ iT−ke→i for all valid numbers in O(N2)O(N2). In fact, this ends up being N4/12N4/12 total operations if optimized. We can then answer each query in O(N2)O(N2) by simply iterating over each chef and finding it's contribution to the answer. Multiplying a matrix O(N2)O(N2) will be explained later.

With some special hacks, it may even be possible to get this to pass.

Key Idea 4 An arbitrary matrix TT has eigenvalues λiλi and their paired eigenvectors v⃗ iv→i. Ignoring how we find eigenvectors and eigenvalues, the point of the two is that they are pairs such that Tv⃗ i=v⃗ iλiTv→i=v→iλi. Notably, given a eigenvector and its respective eigenvalue, we can calculate Tk⋅v⃗ iTk⋅v→i in O(Nlogk)O(Nlog⁡k).

In our case, the transition matrix TT is a diagonal matrix. A basic result in linear algebra is that the eigenvalues of a diagonal matrix is exactly the numbers on it's diagonal. In our case, this means that λi=iλi=i for 1≤i≤n1≤i≤n. Finding the eigenvectors is left as an exercise to the reader. We henceforth denote the eigenvector paired with the ii'th eigenvalue as v⃗ iv→i.

Key Idea 5 Decompose e⃗ ie→i into a linear combination of eigenvectors. This can be precalculated in O(N3)O(N3). Let's denote another vector c⃗ ic→i as this linear combination i.e. c⃗ ic→i satisfies e⃗ i=∑v⃗ ic⃗ i,je→i=∑v→ic→i,j. In fact, this is almost all we need. Let's return to to a=Tk∑di≤kT−die⃗ iaia=Tk∑di≤kT−die→iai (the second part can be trivially calculated). We can calculate almost "separately" for each eigenvector jj. In fact, the contribution of the jj'th eigenvector from the ii'th chef after the kk'th day is λkjci,jaiλ−dijλjkci,jaiλj−di. Let's store ci,jaiλ−jdici,jaiλj−di in a segment tree/BIT on didi. We can extract in O(NlogN)O(Nlog⁡N) total or O(logN)O(log⁡N) for a single eigenvector then find the total contribution by multiplying by λkjλjk and using prefix sums on the eigenvector to extract the relevant range [l,r][l,r]. We can also process O(NlogN)O(NlogN) per update if didi stays constant trivially. This is more than enough to pass, but a further optimization is that the array dd only changes at most O(N)O(N) time (whenever a new element becomes positive). So, we can simply rebuild our segment tree/BIT in O(N)O(N) to reach O(N3)O(N3) complexity.

Final complexity: O(N3+QNlogN)O(N3+QNlog⁡N).

It's also worth noting that several nonoptimal solutions can pass. We attempted to cut off any solutions that explicitly use matrix multiplication. However, solutions with complexity such as O(QN2)O(QN2) can pass in 64 bit C++ if only additions are used. The only way we found to do only additions was by making the same eigenvector reduction, so we were not too worried. It seems impossible to fail such solutions while maintaining a reasonable TL.

Tlatoani
5 years ago, show #

Tlatoani

← Rev. 4 →  
+121

Alternate solution to 1540D - Inverse Inversions

(Read the first nontrivial paragraph of the editorial before reading this alternate solution)

Let pr(k)=xpr(k)=x denote that of the numbers p(1),…,p(r)p(1),…,p(r) in sorted order, p(k)p(k) is equal to the xxth of these numbers. We will take a decomposition strategy just as the editorial does, though our strategy will be different. We will divide [1,n][1,n] into blocks of length bb. For each block covering some interval [l,r][l,r], we will store pr(k)pr(k) for each k∈[l,r]k∈[l,r] in sorted order.

This means that for any kk, if we know pr(k)pr(k) for some block [l,r][l,r], then we can determine pr′(k)pr′(k) for the block [l′,r′][l′,r′] immediately to the right by binary searching on the numbers stored for [l′,r′][l′,r′]. Therefore, we can perform queries in O(nblgb)O(nblg⁡b).

We now need to figure out updates. There are probably simple ways to perform updates in O(blgb)O(blg⁡b), but this yields an overall runtime of O(qn−−√lgn)O(qnlg⁡n) which is too slow.

Therefore, we can instead store each block as a segment tree. For each range [l,r][l,r] in the segment tree we store the same thing we store for the whole block: pr(k)pr(k) for each k∈[l,r]k∈[l,r] in sorted order.

We then have to quickly merge two intervals. We can merge two intervals of length λλ in O(λlgλ)O(λlg⁡λ) by doing binary search just as we did above, but this still only yields O(blgb)O(blg⁡b) update overall. However, these λλ binary searches can be optimized using two pointers to O(λ)O(λ), making the overall update O(b)O(b).

We thus have O(nblgb)O(nblg⁡b) query and O(b)O(b) update. Therefore, we can choose b=nlgn−−−−−√b=nlg⁡n to attain an overall runtime of O(qnlgn−−−−−√)O(qnlg⁡n) just as the editorial does.

Submission in Kotlin

Submission in C++

It is interesting to note that this solution is quite fast. At the time of writing this update, the C++ version is the fastest correct submission (and runs under 1 second!) and the Kotlin version is faster than the vast majority of submissions.

Paging ecnerwala to explain his solution to D1E if he'd like. It seems offline?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    ecnerwala
    5 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    ecnerwala
    5 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +40

    My solution is O(KN3+QN)O(KN3+QN). I just precomputed the coefficient of each aiai for each prefix-range for each number of days since person ii becomes positive (only 10001000 possible days) in O(N⋅K⋅N2)O(N⋅K⋅N2), and then summed up the appropriate ones to answer each query in O(N)O(N). It's written in the offline style to use only O(KN)O(KN) memory at a time (grouped by aiai) instead of O(KN2)O(KN2).

    My passing submission is just KN3/6KN3/6 instead of the KN3KN3 I submitted in contest :'(

    If you guys wanted to prevent this, KK could've been much higher, like 1e181e18.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      ijxjdjd
      5 years ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      ijxjdjd
      5 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +19

      :( I knew of this solution (it’s why ML is tight) but I didn’t realize that it could be done offline with small memory. Of course KK higher is obvious solution but main issue is that the extra modulos from binary exponentiation make it very hard to pass in Java without allowing other unoptimal solutions through such as precomputing inverses of the matrix. Probably k=105k=105 would’ve been a better choice. Thanks.

      → Reply

      No, that's not what you did. Can someone tell me the meaning of this line in problem Div2D/Div1B

Note that, until reaching, l every possible process still has the same probability of reaching b before a. Therefore, we can assume that the process has reached l and calculate the probability from there.

    Here's what it's trying to say:

    • »
      »
      »
      parveen1981
      5 years ago, show (+2) #
      »
      »
      »
      parveen1981
      5 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      So, we mark lca first (of course). But why wouldn't it affect the final probability of reaching b before a? I mean, why is it sufficient to calculate the probability after marking lca?

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        ehnryx
        5 years ago, show (+1) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        ehnryx
        5 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        +19

        Before marking the lca, there is no way to make more progress towards bb than aa or vice versa. The subset of marked vertices also does not change the probability of moving towards aa or bb after reaching the lca because we're choosing uniformly at random and exactly two vertices are of interest.

        → Reply

OleschY
5 years ago, show (+4) #
»
OleschY
5 years ago, hide # |
← Rev. 3 →  
+30

So, my Solution for Div1 Problem B / Div2 Problem D / 1540B — Tree Array:

Chose two Nodes AA and BB with A>BA>B.

First DFS: Find the path from AA to BB. I call it pathppathp. On pathppathp mark the distance to BB for each node.

Second DFS: For each remaining node NN find the shortest path to pathppathp. It will hit it at some node of the pathppathp which has some distance DD marked on it. We mark NN with DD. (See comment below for image.)

Calculation: For each node NN we can calculate PiPi. PiPi is the probability to reach Node BB before we reach Node AA. We sum PiPi for each node. PiPi is also the probability, that the pair of Nodes AA and BB with starting node NN will contribute to the inversion sum.

Iteration: We need to repeat this for each pair AA and BB. In the end we divide the answer by nn, the amount of nodes (the probability to start with Node NN).

This algorithm is O(N3)O(N3). See my Solution 120603369

How to calculate P_i

I wrote myself a small helper DP-program to find the regularities. Let DD be the Distance between AA and BB and dd be the distance from the node NN to BB. My educated guess was: Pi=∑d−1i=0(D−1i)2D−1Pi=∑i=0d−1(D−1i)2D−1

Helper Program

It checks for a path of some length for each Intervall [A,B][A,B] which is already visited, what the probability to reach one node before the other is. 

#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

int solve(int n)
{
    vector<vector<double>> dp(n,  vector<double>(n, 0));


    for(int i = 0; i < n; ++i)
    {
        dp[0][i] = 1;
        dp[i][n - 1] = 0;
    }
    dp[0][n - 1] = -1;
    for(int w = n; w >= 0; w--)
    {
        for(int i = 1; i + w < n - 1; i++)
        {
            int l = i;
            int r = w + i;
            dp[l][r] = 0.5 * (dp[l - 1][r] + dp[l][r + 1]);
        }
    }
    cout << n << "\n\n";
    for(int i = 0; i < n; i++)
    {
        cout << dp[i][i]*(1<<(n-2)) << "\n";

    }
    cout << "\n\n\n\n";
    return 0;
}

//======================
// Technical stuff
//======================

int main()
{
    int ntest = 12;
    for(int test = 2; test < ntest; ++test)
    {
        solve(test);
    }
    return 0;
}
→ Reply

        Mrajaryan
        5 years ago, show (+1) #

        Mrajaryan
        5 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        what does the dp states mean in your helper program? I am unable to understand. Can you please explain?

        → Reply
        • »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          OleschY
          5 years ago, show #
          »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          OleschY
          5 years ago, hide # ^ |
           
          0

          You have Nodes 11 through NN, neighbouring IDs are connected. The state dp[l][r]dp[l][r] is the probability, that node NN will be reached before node 11 with all the nodes ll through rr marked already. Obviously dp[1][x]=0dp[1][x]=0 and dp[x][N]=1dp[x][N]=1 (dp[1][N]dp[1][N] can't happen). The recurrence is dp[l][r]=(dp[l−1][r]+dp[l][r+1])/2dp[l][r]=(dp[l−1][r]+dp[l][r+1])/2

          → Reply

I can't grasp the editorial of Div 2 D/ Div 1 B. Can somebody provide a more intuitive explanation?

→ Reply

    → Reply
  • »
    »
    ehnryx
    5 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    ehnryx
    5 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +25

    Step 1. use linearity of expectation. The answer is

    ∑a<bP(b appears before a).∑a<bP(b appears before a).

    Step 2: Observe that if we start by marking a vertex cc on the path between aa and bb, and suppose the next marked vertex on the path is dd. Then, the probability that dd is between cc and aa and the probability that dd is between cc and bb are both 1/21/2. This is because there are only two choices for dd and we're choosing uniformly at random. This means that the answer only depends on dist(c,a)dist(c,a) and dist(c,b)dist(c,b).

    Step 3: run a dp to calculate the probability that we mark bb before aa given dist(c,a)dist(c,a) and dist(c,b)dist(c,b).

    → Reply
»

include<bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

int main(){ ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0); cin.tie(0); cout.tie(0); int t; cin >> t; while(t--){ int n; cin >> n; long long arr[n]; for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){ cin >> arr[i]; } long long cnt = 0; for(int i = 0; i < n — 1; i++){ for(int j = arr[i] — 2 — i; j < n; j += arr[i]){ if(j < 0 || j >= n) continue; else{ if((arr[i] * arr[j] == i + j + 2) && (j > i)) cnt++; } } } cout << cnt << "\n"; } return 0; }

/* Accepted code A different approach using arrays (as I don't know what vectors are, haven't read that) I hope this is a optimal approach. Any suggestions related to this are whole-heartedly welcomed. Also, please guide me how could I have optimized the code to a much extent. Thanks in advance! Keep programming! */

  • »
    »
    noneofyourbusiness
    5 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    noneofyourbusiness
    5 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +5

    Simply running two loops and checking every case would give a TLE. So, we might want to minimize the number of operations. For this, we would only consider the cases where the sum of indices is a multiple of an element.

    For this, we would first create two loops, one within the other, first loop iterating i from 0 to (n — 1) with an incrementation of 1. By observation, we can see that the first index for which the sum of indices will be a multiple of arr[i] is (arr[i] — 2 — i).

    So, in the nested loop we will run j = (arr[i] — 2 — i) till (n — 1) with an incrementation of arr[i]. We would ignore the cases where j < 0 or j >= n.

    Finally, we need to check for how many cases this holds (arr[i] * arr[j] = i + j + 2 and j > i).

    Suggestions are welcomed!

    → Reply
»
noneofyourbusiness
5 years ago, show (+5) #
»
noneofyourbusiness
5 years ago, hide # |
 
-64

include<bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

int main(){

ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0); cin.tie(0); cout.tie(0);

int t;

cin >> t;

while(t--){

int n;

cin >> n;

long long arr[n];

for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){

    cin >> arr[i];

}

long long cnt = 0;

for(int i = 0; i < n — 1; i++){

for(int j = arr[i] &mdash; 2 &mdash; i; j < n; j += arr[i]){

    if(j < 0 || j >= n) continue;

    else{

    if((arr[i] * arr[j] == i + j + 2) && (j > i)) cnt++;

    }

}

}

cout << cnt << "\n";

}

return 0;

}

/* Accepted code

A different approach using arrays (as I don't know what vectors are, haven't read that)

I hope this is a optimal approach.

Any suggestions related to this are whole-heartedly welcomed.

Also, please guide me how could I have optimized the code to a much extent.

Thanks in advance!

Keep programming!

*/

willy108
5 years ago, show #
»
willy108
5 years ago, hide # |
 
+24

Div2 B can also be done in O(NsqrtN). We know that for a given pair of indeces i+j < 2n, so any pair that a[i] * a[j] < 2n will have to have one of the two terms be <= sqrt(n) (with some off by one errors of course). So the algorithm is to store an array of pairs [array value, index] and sort that array by the value. If the array value is <= sqrt(2n) we can naively loop over the rest of the array in O(n) time and check (be careful about overcount), and if the value is > sqrt(n), we can ignore it. This works since when a[i] * a[j] < 2n one of a[i] or a[j] has to be <= sqrt(2n) and as a result, every pair will be counted.

→ Reply
»

+6

I don't really understand the need for a recursive function for the stack emptying probabilities in 1540B - Tree Array. I mean given that you have a stack of size n and and m you can basically have an array of size n + m filled with 0s and 1s where 0 at the ith place means that the ith element was taken from the first stack. Any such array which has n 0s and m 1s correspond to one process and it's easy to see that whoever takes the last spot in the array gets emptied later which gives an easy way to calculate the probabilities. Namely (n+m−1n−1)/(n+mn)(n+m−1n−1)/(n+mn) for the first and similar to the other.

      I would guess that there’s no easy closed form. You can evaluate in O(n)O(n) however by counting right up paths from (a,0)(a,0) to (x,y)(x,y) for all aa and multiplying by 2−steps2−steps.

    geekpradd
    5 years ago, show #

    geekpradd
    5 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +9

    The problem with this is that all the possibilities are not equilikely, consider m=2,n=1m=2,n=1 and let 11 denote entries from the stack of size nn. Then the probability of obtaining 100100 is 1/21/2, while obtaining 010010 and 001001 has a probability of 1/41/4. Your approach assumes a uniform prior probability (in which case the answer is indeed 1/31/3 whereas here it is 1/41/4 which is the probability of getting 001001)

    → Reply

Problem Div2C/Div1A, Plz somebody explain 3rd hint. I didn't get why this condition must be true

The sum of the values of edges with positive weight must be ≥ the maximum value in the array.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    shiftyblock
    5 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    shiftyblock
    5 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    I like to think about C this way: The cheapest node is the root, and the most expensive node, X, is the one with the highest value, D. Therefore no matter how we make our edges, we need at least 1 path from node to X with distance D. So let's build 1 single edge of positive weight from 1 to X with weight D.

    Now from node X, all other nodes are <= D. We can use negative edges to go there. Now the problem just becomes "assign as many negative edges as possible" to the rest of the nodes.

    → Reply
»
r57shell
5 years ago, show (+7) #
»
r57shell
5 years ago, hide # |
 
0

In problem 1540B - Tree Array I agree with everything up to:

Once l is reached, we now note that the probability that the process "gets closer" to b is always equal to the probability of getting closer to a.

I agree with this quote if it was about each individual set of marked nodes and single step for them. Because for any individual set of marked nodes, those probabilities is just one over the number of options at the moment. But I don't understand why I should forget about everything else what happens with other parts of tree, because after single step which is neither towards a neither towards b, the number of options (nodes we can mark on next step) may change.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    ijxjdjd
    5 years ago, show (+6) #
    »
    »
    ijxjdjd
    5 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +8

    That is correct, but to see how it stays the same you can think of it inductively. Use strong induction and assume probability is the same no matter what the state of the tree is. Then from (x,y)(x,y) you always have an equal probability of ending up in one of the two states you can transition to because pp is always the same. Every scenario you enter one state, there’s another scenario with the same probability that enters the other state. So, the probability of entering one of the two states is the same as the other, thus 0.50.5. Hopefully that makes things more clear.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      r57shell
      5 years ago, show (+5) #
      »
      »
      »
      r57shell
      5 years ago, hide # ^ |
      ← Rev. 2 →  
      0

      Oh thanks, it's clear now. So, base of induction is when only l reached, and we can show that probability to make step towards a and b is same because for each individual set you can go from l to b instead of going from l into a, using exactly same steps in between (those steps which doesn't change distances to a and b). And similar holds for next steps.

      → Reply

        Can you explain this?

        Assume XX is initially node we chose. Then define a function gg :

        g[a][b][STATE]g[a][b][STATE] = probability to reach a before b while state of the tree we reach is STATESTATE, and aa, bb is length of path.

        follow the image, I can see : g[a][b][STATEx]=14(g[a][b][STATEd]+g[a][b][STATEe]+g[a—1][b][closera]+g[a][b—1][closerb])g[a][b][STATEx]=14(g[a][b][STATEd]+g[a][b][STATEe]+g[a—1][b][closera]+g[a][b—1][closerb])

        It can easy see that the probability can change. Or I wrong in some where?

        • »
          »
          »

          r57shell
          5 years ago, show (+3) #
          »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          r57shell
          5 years ago, hide # ^ |
           
          0

          I'll hide my long explanation under spoiler

          horrible wall of text

          To put things into words, I want to define some things. First, suppose we marked some s1,s2,s3...sks1,s2,s3...sk vertices in exact this order. Then, let P(s)P(s) to be probability to mark them in this sequence. It can be decomposed into P(s)=p1⋅p2⋅p3⋅...⋅pkP(s)=p1⋅p2⋅p3⋅...⋅pk where pi=1/oi−1pi=1/oi−1 where oioi is number of options at step ii — number of opened vertices, except p0=1/np0=1/n, or o0=no0=n.

          Then, let l=LCA(a,b)l=LCA(a,b) where LCALCA is lowest common ancestor. Then, let xvxv to be some sort of 'cost'. For each vv on path between aa and ll it will be distance to ll, and −1−1 everywhere else. Similarly, for each vv on path between bb and ll let yvyv to be distance to ll and −1−1 everywhere else. Let

          A(s)=maxv∈sxvB(s)=maxv∈syvA(s)=maxv∈sxvB(s)=maxv∈syv

          Then A(s)A(s) is equal to how far we reach aa, and B(s)B(s) is equal to how far we reach bb. Let say we are in situation (A(s),B(s))(A(s),B(s)) after marking ss, and therefore situation is pair of numbers. Situation (−1,−1)(−1,−1) corresponds that no vertex is marked on path from aa to bb. Situation (0,0)(0,0) corresponds to only ll marked on path from aa to bb. And situation (1,0)(1,0) corresponds to ll and single vertex towards aa is marked.

          Finally, let say sequence of marks ss reached state (A(s),B(s))(A(s),B(s)) if either A(s)=xskA(s)=xsk or B(s)=yskB(s)=ysk, in other words, last vertex is on path from aa to bb and it did change situation.

          Now, what we want to prove is following:

          ∑sreached(α+1,β)P(s)=∑sreached(α,β+1)P(s)∑sreached(α+1,β)P(s)=∑sreached(α,β+1)P(s)

          For some fixed s1s1, which is root we chosen. And the way we prove it is that ss that is reached (α+1,β)(α+1,β) consists of uu that reached (α,β)(α,β) plus additional steps: ww plus sksk. So s=u..w..sks=u..w..sk where .... is concatenation. It's easy to see what none of vertices from ww is on path from aa to bb. Also, it's easy to see that we could instead of choosing sksk could choose vertex zz towards bb, and it would have same probability. In other words P(s)=P(u..w..z)P(s)=P(u..w..z). And this u..w..zu..w..z should reach state (α,β+1)(α,β+1). So it should be in right side sum of our equation we want to prove.

          So, for any ss from left sum, we can make s′s′ from right sum in unique way, and it will have same P(s)=P(s′)P(s)=P(s′). Similarly, for any ss from right sum, we can make s′s′ from left sum in unique way, and it will have same P(s)=P(s′)P(s)=P(s′). So this is bijection, and each element from left sum corresponds to element from right sum with same value, so those sums should have same value. Therefore it's equality.

          Initially I wanted to trim ss from (α+1,β)(α+1,β) to (α,β)(α,β) and show similar thing based on following sum:

          ∑sreached(α,β)P(s)∑sreached(α,β)P(s)

          Which is probability to reach state (α,β)(α,β), but luckily it didn't required. I'll use notation P(reach(α,β))P(reach(α,β)) for this probability.

          Thing that is not covered though: why this equality can be translated into probability =1/2=1/2? Well, from (α,β)(α,β) you eventually will reach either (α+1,β)(α+1,β) or (α,β+1)(α,β+1), so you can see this as example of Law of total probability. P(reach(α,β))=P(reach(α+1,β))+P(reach(α,β+1))P(reach(α,β))=P(reach(α+1,β))+P(reach(α,β+1)), because events (α+1,β)(α+1,β) and (α,β+1)(α,β+1) are disjoint events given (α,β)(α,β) is reached. And we proved they have equal probability so P(reach(α,β))=2⋅P(reach(α+1,β))P(reach(α,β))=2⋅P(reach(α+1,β)), so P(reach(α+1,β))=1/2⋅P(reach(α,β))P(reach(α+1,β))=1/2⋅P(reach(α,β)) which we actually use.

          → Reply

            Oh, sorry, there is one missing part. We proved P(reached(α+1,β))=P(reached(α,β+1))P(reached(α+1,β))=P(reached(α,β+1)) given (α,β)(α,β) is reached, but this is actually what we need. This given condition is what I missed. Without given we could reach (α+1,β)(α+1,β) from reaching (α+1,β−1)(α+1,β−1).

              But unfortunately, it's wrong (may be, or I still missunderstanding)

                P(A<B)P(A<B) in your terms is exactly what we need (if a = A and b = B).

                I don't understand last formula, everything else looks fine.

                And to find P(A<B)P(A<B) we use fact above and calculate all possible ways to reach aa earlier than bb we use (α,β)(α,β) states using my notation: you either get αα equal to dist to aa when ββ = 0, or ββ = 1, or 2, or 3...

                P(A<B)==∑i=0dist(b,l)P(reach(dist(a,l),i)givenreached(dist(a,l)−1,i))=∑i=0dist(b,l)P(reached(dist(a,l)−1,i))⋅12P(A<B)==∑i=0dist(b,l)P(reach(dist(a,l),i)givenreached(dist(a,l)−1,i))=∑i=0dist(b,l)P(reached(dist(a,l)−1,i))⋅12

                Or you can rephrase task into other task with two kind of balls. What probability to remove all balls of one kind earlier than other, if you pick one or other kind of ball with probability 1/2.

    ← Rev. 6 →  
    0

    A[i]*A[j] == (i+j). let's say (i+j) = k, we can say that A[i] and A[j] are the factors of k and the question is now reduced to finding the factors of k.

    1. Range of k: for a pair to form, we must have atleast two elements, so for size = 2, min value of k = 1+2 =3 and for size = n, max value would be n+n-1 i.e 2*n-1
    2. for each k run a loop till sqrt(k) and check whether the factors present in the given array and the given conditions holds or not

    (https://codeforces.com/contest/1541/submission/120654656)

    → Reply

    Bro can u explain this soln????? i thought of taking all pairs that give negative edges except for the adjacent pairs.... bt getting wrong ans in 3rd 4th test case.....

    while(n>2) { sum-=(n-2)*(llabs(a[j]-a[i])); n--; // n = size i++; // i = 0 j--; // j = n-1 } cout<<sum<<endl;

      praveen_naik__

      »
      »
      praveen_naik__
      5 years ago, hide # ^ |
      ← Rev. 2 →  
      0

      My solution is simple. First sort the array.

      Then start connecting adjacent values with their differences. This way sum of all edges with positive weight will be same as the sum of adjacent differences in the array.

      After that start making negative edges for every i. So each i will have i negative edges. Where negative weight is same as -(arr[i] — arr[j]).

      Instead of search it for every j I have formula as (prefixsum till i) — arr[i]*i

      → Reply

        0

        can you tell me why are we sorting the array for a particular node call it x we need to add a negative weight from x to 1 , x to 2 x to 3 till x to x-1 keeping in mind the the path sum doesn't become negative so why are we sorting the array

          5 years ago, show (+2) #
          »

          »
          »
          »
          praveen_naik__
          5 years ago, hide # ^ |
           
          0

          We are sorting values only once so as to connect neighboring nodes with minimum values,i.e. difference b/w consecutive values.
          From this sum of positive edges will be minimum.

For DIV 2C/1A can anyone explain with this test case N = 6 and D = 0 1 2 3 2 3. What are the edges that we can have with their weights?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Zemrith
    5 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    Zemrith
    5 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0

    Hello! The answer would be -18.

    Diagram:

    Notice that once you sort the distances, the adjacent nodes have no effect on your final answer. But you can add negative edges as long as they are not adjacent, resulting in such a diagram. Hence you can use prefix sums to solve the problem. (if x nodes came before this, for each node, the answer to add is (x-1)*curr value — csum of first (x-1) nodes).

    Hope that made sense!

    → Reply

      »
      »
      goingOn
      5 years ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      goingOn
      5 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      Thank you Zemrith for so much detail explanation and the solution too it helped me a lot.

      → Reply
  • »
    »
    Forrest_Gump
    5 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    Forrest_Gump
    5 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    first sort the array they will from non negative weight edges. 0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 2 -> 3 -> 3 so the non negative weights will be 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0. form here greedily build most negative weights(backward edges) such that there are no negative cycles.

    → Reply
  • »
    »
    ilidar
    5 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    ilidar
    5 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    First you can sort D and get: N = 6, D = [0, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3]

    Now calculate the diffs:

    diffs = [1, 1, 0, 1, 0]

    The edges for this graph could be something like this:

       1     1     0     1     0      <- forward edges
1 <-> 2 <-> 3 <-> 4 <-> 5 <-> 6
  -1    -1     0    -1     0      <- backward edges

    Now you have to add more negatives edges, and you could do this by choosing some i and j, i < j and add an edges from j to i, and the weight will be sum of the values from diff[i] to diff[j].

    Another way to think about this is: look at D array, it represents distances between adjacent nodes, all we have to do is add all of the edges with length 2, then all of the edges of length 3, ..., all of the edges of length N - 1.

    So, for our case we would have these edges

    a b   W
-------
1 2 - 1 
2 3 - 1 
3 4 - 0    <- adjacent forward edges
4 5 - 1
5 6 - 0

2 1 - -1 
3 2 - -1 
4 3 - 0    <- adjacent backward edges
5 4 - -1
6 5 - 0

3 1 - -2 
4 2 - -1   
5 3 - -1    <- edges of length 2
6 4 - -1

4 1 - -2 
5 2 - -2   <- edges of length 3
6 3 - -1    

5 1 - -3 
6 2 - -2   <- edges of length 4

6 1 - -3   <- edges of length 5
    → Reply

      5 years ago, show #

AquaBlaze
5 years ago, show (+3) #
»
AquaBlaze
5 years ago, hide # |
← Rev. 7 →  
+18

Could someone please provide a more strict intuition or insight of Div2D/Div1B of why "the actual probability p does not matter"? The intuition in the editorial is still alien to me of why those choices of not progess toward to either stacks (and probability 'p' also changes from time to time too) doesn't matter.

Update: Here is the intuition I came up with (The strict proof can be found in the comment of the author below)

Let dpi,jdpi,j = the probability of emptying the first stack (which now have ii things left) before the second stack (which now have jj things left) in some states of the current tree.

now, we will try break this dpi,jdpi,j down into the sum of dpi−1,jdpi−1,j and dpi,j−1dpi,j−1

We will try to illustrate this with trying to split and color, either red or blue, a stick of length 11. The length of the sticks representing the 'probability', and the color of the sticks will represent dpi−1,jdpi−1,j(red) or dpi,j−1dpi,j−1(blue), depending on the color.

Suppose in the current state, we have probability pp for choosing to pop each stacks, and the rest 1−2p1−2p of doing nothing. The picture will look like this:

We will split the stick equally(*) into several sticks of length pp, and then color two of them red and blue. (* We can split it evenly because in the original problem, pp is in the form 1number of candidate unmark nodes1number of candidate unmark nodes ) Now, the remaining sticks represent the state of dpi,jdpi,j again (in some other state of the entire tree, so might be in some different pp). That means we will split those sticks similary.

The key observations is:

1) We know that, in the original problem, if we keep picking nodes that aren't progressing toward the target nodes, we will run out of nodes eventually and finally choose the two nodes. That means, all the sticks will eventually colored into 'red' and 'blue'.

2) When we split a stick into several smaller equal length sticks, we will color two of them into red and blue. Those two sticks always have the same length. That means, the total length of blue sticks and the total length of red sticks will be equal in the end.

Analogically, that means, eventually, dpi,jdpi,j will split into dpi−1,jdpi−1,j and dpi,j−1dpi,j−1 evenly, no matter pp might be or the state of tree of dpi,jdpi,j might be. Therefore, dpi,j=12⋅(dpi−1,j+dpi,j−1)dpi,j=12⋅(dpi−1,j+dpi,j−1)

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    AquaBlaze
    5 years ago, show (+2) #
    »
    »
    AquaBlaze
    5 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +10

    Let dpi,jdpi,j = the probability of emptying the first stack (which now have ii things left) before the second stack (which now have jj things left), with having arbitary probability 0<p≤0.50<p≤0.5 of chosing to pick the top of each stack (and 1−2p1−2p for doing nothing). Then

    dpi,j=∫0.50x⋅(dpi−1,j+dpi,j−1)+(1−2x)⋅dpi,jdxdpi,j=∫00.5x⋅(dpi−1,j+dpi,j−1)+(1−2x)⋅dpi,jdx

    Solving the equation, we get dpi,j=16⋅(dpi−1,j+dpi,j−1)dpi,j=16⋅(dpi−1,j+dpi,j−1) What is the mistake in this logic?

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      ijxjdjd
      5 years ago, show (+1) #
      »
      »
      »
      ijxjdjd
      5 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +13

      The biggest issue with this logic is that it's assuming pp is arbitrary chosen from a certain state. While pp can be anything in the world, it is always an exact number from a certain state, hence why an integral is wrong.

      As a different type of intuition, you can think, "is it more likely to reach (i−1,j)(i−1,j) than state (i,j−1)(i,j−1)"? and vice versa. For me at least, I don't see how it's possible for either of those questions to be true, so they should be equal.

      If you're looking for a more rigorously correct dpdp, it would look something like this.

      Proof

      Let dpi,j,Sdpi,j,S denote the probability of reaching some node ii distance away before some node jj distance away where SS is a representation of the entire state of of the process (not necessarily an integer). I think you already understood why we can assume the lcalca is already reached. We aim to show that SS does not matter in our calculation. Assume inductively that SS does not matter. So, we can assume that states (i−1,j)(i−1,j) and states (i,j−1)(i,j−1) are irrelevant to SS. Hence, the part we need to care about is (1−2p)dpi,j,S→Sa(1−2p)dpi,j,S→Sa. A way of thinking about this part of the transition is moving through the collection of SS with the state (i,j)(i,j). Obviously, the dpdp is a DAGDAG because no state SS can reach another. Each bounce takes a certain probability pp which is just multiplied in the current path. So, for each state SS with state (i,j)(i,j), we have a certain probability to reach it by simply calculating dpdp along a DAG as is traditional. Then, from those states of (i,j)(i,j) you transition to (i,j−1)(i,j−1) with an equal probability (i−1,j)(i−1,j). So, they have to be equal. Finally, this argument holds for any initial state SS that you reach, so we can conclude that, from any state SS with a state of (i,j)(i,j), the probability of transitions to (i−1,j)(i−1,j) and (i,j−1)(i,j−1) are always exactly 0.50.5.

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        AquaBlaze
        5 years ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        AquaBlaze
        5 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        I see, thank you so much!

        → Reply

I am getting wrong ans . could someone tell me where the code make differene ( [yestrday competiton problem .

`](https://codeforces.com/contest/1541/problem/B).

int main( ) {
    clock_t begin = clock();
    file_i_o();
    // Write your code here....
    int t;
    cin>>t;
    while(t-- ){
        int n;
        cin>>n;
        vector<pair<int,int>>v;
        v.push_back({0,0});
        loop(i,0,n) {
            int x;
            cin>>x;
            v.pb({x,i+1});

        } 
        sort(v.begin()+1,v.end());
        int count =0;
        for(int i=1 ; i<=n;i++) {
            for(ll j=i+1;j<=n;j++) {
                ll left = v[i].first * v[j].first;
                ll right = v[i].second + v[j].second;

                if(left == right) count++;
                if(left > 2*n ) break;
            }
        }
        cout<<count<<endl;
        
    }
→ Reply
  • »
    »
    LUL____SEPLED1305
    5 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    LUL____SEPLED1305
    5 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0

    v[i].first * v[j].first can create overflow. So, you need to convert them to long long by using

    ll left = 1LL * v[i].first * v[j].first;

    instead and it would pass.

    (Simply save the value in long long won't help. You need to convert them to long long before doing multiplication. 1LL* is one way)

    → Reply
»

Hi, in problem Div1.B/Div2.D; I can't wrap my head around F[x][y]=F[x−1][y]/2+F[x][y−1]/2F[x][y]=F[x−1][y]/2+F[x][y−1]/2. Why is it not F[x][y]=F[x−1][y+1]/2+F[x+1][y−1]/2F[x][y]=F[x−1][y+1]/2+F[x+1][y−1]/2, can someone please explain to me why is my transition wrong and/or why is the aforementioned transition correct?

    +1

    x and y is the distance left for each side right? So, if you take one out, it won't make sense to add that one to the other side since the distance should be either x-1 and y or x and y-1.

code forces round 728 div2

    0

    If i am using #define node pair<int,int> it is getting accepted but when i am using typedef pair<int,int> node; it is giving wrong answer

    Why this is happening ?? Is it a bug??

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      LUL____SEPLED1305
      5 years ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      LUL____SEPLED1305
      5 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +8

      Not really sure why this is happening.

      However, I think the problem is the position of #define int long long. So, for #define pair<int,int> node it seems that compiler change node -> pair<int,int> -> pair<long long, long long>. However, when you do typedef, it still keeps in pair<int, int> which creates an overflow problem later on.

      I did try moving #define int long long up above typedef and the code pass. So, my best guess is #define int long long only replace int after that position with long long. Thus, node is still pair<int, int> in the typedef solution, while node is changed to pair<long long, long long> in the second solution.

      → Reply

    Check the definition of bi again dude. bi here means number of elements greater than pi. So to get ci, which is number of elements smaller than pi, you need i-bi.

Can someone explain how you can find the LCA for each pair so quick? Iterating through every root is and then considering every pair is already N^3

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    PurpleCrayon
    5 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    PurpleCrayon
    5 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +25

    There are a couple ways you could do it:

    1. Just use standard binary lifting (initialize once for each root). This runs in O(n3logn)O(n3log⁡n), and should pass under the given constraints. You could also just use O(1)O(1) lca using an rmq over an euler tour.
    2. You could use a version of dp, where dp[a][b]=lca(a,b)dp[a][b]=lca(a,b). If the depth of aa is greater than the depth of bb, dp[a][b]=dp[parent[a]][b]dp[a][b]=dp[parent[a]][b], otherwise dp[a][b]=dp[a][parent[b]]dp[a][b]=dp[a][parent[b]]. The base cases are dp[a][a]=adp[a][a]=a for all aa. This runs in O(n3)O(n3).
    3. You could extend this idea and do the main solution's dp directly on the tree (without ever worrying about lca's). The recurrence is equivalent to the main solution (dp[a][b]=dp[parent[a]][b]+dp[a][parent[b]]2dp[a][b]=dp[parent[a]][b]+dp[a][parent[b]]2 with the base cases being one node is an ancestor of the other.
    → Reply

0

Div1D can be done in O(nn−−√)O(nn). We can use square root decomposition to replace all BITs in tutorial. Since a value in a non-updated position changes by at most one and all values change in the same direction, the full recomputation is only needed in the updated position and we can perform an incremental change in O(1)O(1) for values in each non-updated positions.

Code

→ Reply

ijxjdjd in problem Tree array you said that Fixing a given root r, the expected value of the entire process is obviously the sum of the expected values for a fixed root divided by n.

why we divide by n at the end ?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    ijxjdjd
    5 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    ijxjdjd
    5 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    The calculation is independent based on whichever node that you choose first (it becomes the “root”). Initially you choose one of nn nodes with equal probability so you divide by nn at the end after you’ve summed up the independent expected value after choosing the node ii initially.

    → Reply
»

For 1D:

Then, you can increment all of those suffixes in the next array using another BIT, which would lead to O(nn−−√+qn−−√logn)O(nn+qnlog⁡n), as queries are O((n/B)logn)O((n/B)log⁡n) and updates are O(Blogn)O(Blog⁡n), which will probably be too slow.

It can be made to fit within the TL (after significant suffering).
